did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid learning technique for melanoma prediction and classification. The method involves feature extraction using Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and thresholding, initial prediction using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with hyperparameters, and advanced prediction using an ensemble-based CNN algorithm. The study was implemented on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, achieving 97.4% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a hybrid learning technique for melanoma prediction and classification. It details specific methodology (GLCM, CNN, ensemble-based CNN), implementation on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, and reports new empirical results (97.4% accuracy). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICETEA), it contains original research contributions rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Prediction and Classification for Melanoma using Hybrid Learning Technique and Multi-Features
journal: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Technologies in Engineering Applications (ICETEA)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid learning technique for the prediction and classification of melanoma to improve diagnostic accuracy. The methodology involves feature extraction using Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and thresholding, followed by an initial prediction stage using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with hyperparameters. An advanced prediction stage is then performed using an ensemble-based CNN algorithm to enhance classification performance. The proposed method was implemented and evaluated using the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, which contains clinical images and patient data. The experimental results demonstrated that the hybrid approach achieved an overall accuracy of 97.4%, outperforming standard CNN models without ensemble learning. This indicates that combining feature selection, CNN, and ensemble learning effectively aids in the early identification of melanoma.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary focus of the study is the prediction and classification of melanoma. The paper mentions subtypes including nodular, lentigo, superficial spreading, and acral lentiginous melanoma. It was evaluated on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.
Included as one of the three classes evaluated in the experiment using the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. Accuracy for Nevus was reported as 96% with ensemble learning.
Included as one of the three classes evaluated in the experiment using the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. Accuracy for Seborrheic Keratosis was reported as 92% with ensemble learning.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. The model predicts discrete classes: Melanoma (Mel), Nevus (Nev), and Seborrheic Keratosis (SK). The study reports accuracy metrics for these specific classes (e.g., 97.4% overall accuracy, 96% for Nevus, 92% for Seborrheic Keratosis with ensemble learning) rather than continuous numeric outcomes.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A skin lesion dataset composed of patient data and clinical images collected from smartphones. It contains 2298 images, 1641 lesions, and 1373 patients with 26 features including patient ID, lesion ID, smoke details, drinking details, age, gender, and cancer history.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid learning technique (CNN with hyperparameters and ensemble-based CNN).
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed method.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the PAD-UFES-20 dataset into training, validation, and testing subsets.
To evaluate and compare the performance of the proposed hybrid learning method against standard CNN models and other state-of-the-art methods.
To assess the stability and improvement of prediction accuracy over multiple runs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy and error rates over 100 iterations.

Baseline CNN (without ensemble).notes: Mentioned in introduction as state-of-the-art comparison, but specific class breakdown not provided in results table.

PAD-UFES-20.notes: The proposed hybrid method achieved 97.4% overall accuracy with ensemble learning, outperforming the initial prediction stage (94% accuracy) and other state-of-the-art methods listed in Table II. The initial prediction accuracy for Nev, SK, and Mel classes was 86%, 78%, and 89% respectively before iteration improvements.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Learning Technique comprising: 1) Feature extraction using Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) and Otsu's thresholding; 2) Initial prediction using a custom CNN with hyperparameters; 3) Advanced prediction using an ensemble-based CNN algorithm with a meta-learner.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) with features: Contrast, Dissimilarity, Homogeneity, Correlation. Thresholding method: Otsu’s.

initial_prediction_cnn.description: Custom CNN structure with hyperparameters for image segmentation and initial prediction.
layers: Convolutional layers (second convolutional layer converts pixel values to single values)
initial_prediction_cnn.input_processing: Images rescaled from [0,255] to [0,1]. Grayscale conversion using formula: Igay = 0.2989R + 0.5870G + 0.1140B.

advanced_prediction_ensemble.aggregation_method: Meta-learner combines predictions from base learners (weighted majority vote or weighted average based on model weights derived from error rates).

training_details.optimization: Hyperparameter tuning for the initial CNN; Ensemble boosting/weighting for the meta-learner

preprocessing_techniques: Image scaling/rescaling (rescaling pixel values from [0,255] to [0,1])
Normalization (using formula I' = I - min(I) / max(I) - min(I))
Data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zooming, and adjustment)
Feature extraction using Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) with features: Contrast, Dissimilarity, Homogeneity, Correlation
Thresholding using Otsu’s method for segmentation
Dataset splitting (70:15:15 ratio for training, validation, and testing)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as part of the data augmentation techniques; specific angles or probabilities not reported.
Applied as part of the data augmentation techniques; specific directions or probabilities not reported.
Applied as part of the data augmentation techniques; specific zoom factors or ranges not reported.
Applied as part of the data augmentation techniques; specific parameters not reported.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid learning technique, which combines feature selection (GLCM), initial prediction using a CNN with hyperparameters, and advanced prediction using an ensemble-based CNN algorithm, achieved an overall accuracy of 97.4% on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset.

The hybrid method outperformed standard CNN models without ensemble learning (94% accuracy) and other state-of-the-art methods such as Yilmaz and Trocan (92%), Dutta et al. (87%), and Khan et al. (90.7%).

Ensemble learning significantly improved classification accuracy for individual classes compared to the initial prediction stage: Nevus accuracy increased from 86% to 96%, Seborrheic Keratosis from 78% to 92%, and Melanoma from 89% to 97.4% over 100 iterations.
The study acknowledges that previous methods relying on a single prediction stage had unreliable accuracy, whereas the multi-stage hybrid approach provides better prediction and classification.

The authors identify limitations in the current study's reliance on a limited set of features and datasets, noting that skin cancer diagnosis is complicated by morphological distinctions and image discrepancies (angle, zoom, lighting).
Future work is directed toward combining multiple datasets to increase dataset size and incorporating transfer and federated learning to further improve accuracy.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset, which consists of images collected from smartphones; this introduces potential bias regarding image quality, lighting, and angle compared to standardized clinical dermoscopic images.
The paper acknowledges that the current method relies on a limited set of features and datasets, which may restrict generalizability to broader clinical populations.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the single internal dataset (PAD-UFES-20), limiting the assessment of robustness across different data distributions.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between the proposed hybrid method and baseline models, relying instead on accuracy metrics over 100 iterations.

The dataset split is reported as 60% training, 20% validation, and 20% testing in the results section, which differs from the 70:15:15 ratio mentioned in the preprocessing list, creating ambiguity in the experimental setup.

The proposed method achieves high accuracy (97.4%) but is evaluated against older state-of-the-art methods (e.g., Yilmaz 2020, Dutta 2021) rather than more recent benchmarks, potentially overstating the relative performance gain.
The ensemble component uses a meta-learner with up to 15 base CNN learners; the computational cost and inference latency implications for clinical deployment are not discussed.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; specifically, it is unclear if patient-level separation was enforced during the 60/20/20 split, which is critical to prevent overoptimistic results if multiple images from the same patient appear in both training and testing sets.
